news_summary: Today's environmental breakthroughs include affordable clean hydrogen production, nature-based climate justice solutions for vulnerable communities, and new insights into ancient geological processes shaping Central Asia.
news_summary_fr: Parmi les avancées environnementales actuelles, on peut citer la production d'hydrogène propre à un coût abordable, des solutions de justice climatique fondées sur la nature pour les communautés vulnérables, ainsi que de nouvelles connaissances sur les processus géologiques anciens qui ont façonné l'Asie centrale.
news_summary_es: Entre los avances medioambientales actuales se incluyen la producción asequible de hidrógeno limpio, soluciones de justicia climática basadas en la naturaleza para las comunidades vulnerables y nuevos conocimientos sobre los antiguos procesos geológicos que dieron forma a Asia Central.

articles.title: Scientists just unlocked a cheaper way to make clean hydrogen fuel
articles.summary: Researchers have developed a durable new catalyst that produces clean hydrogen without relying on expensive platinum metals. The breakthrough could make renewable hydrogen fuel cheaper, more efficient, and easier to scale for real-world energy use.
articles.content:
Renewable energy sources can cut harmful emissions, reduce reliance on fossil fuels, and improve efficiency. However, many clean energy technologies remain expensive because they depend on costly materials such as platinum group metals (PGM) and require efficient ways to store energy for later use.

Researchers at Washington University in St. Louis are working on a possible solution. A team led by Gang Wu, professor of energy, environmental & chemical engineering in the McKelvey School of Engineering, has developed a new catalyst designed for an anion-exchange membrane water electrolyzer (AEMWE). This technology uses electricity from renewable sources to split water into hydrogen and oxygen, producing clean hydrogen fuel in the process.

New Platinum-Free Hydrogen Catalyst

Wu's group focused on replacing expensive platinum-based materials commonly used in hydrogen production systems. Their approach uses renewable electricity generated from sunlight, wind, or water to power the separation of hydrogen from water molecules.

"Going from water to hydrogen is a very desirable way we are able to store energy for different applications," Wu said. "Hydrogen itself can be used as an energy carrier and is useful for different chemical industries and manufacturing."

To build the catalyst, the researchers combined rhenium phosphide (Re 2 P) and molybdenum phosphide (MoP). Together, the two materials created a highly effective composite that improved the hydrogen extraction process. The rhenium component helped hydrogen attach to and release from the catalyst surface, while the molybdenum sped up the splitting of water in the alkaline electrolyte.

Durable Performance for Clean Energy

The team paired the new catalyst with a nickel iron anode and found that the system performed better than a leading state-of-the-art cathode, including one based on PGM materials. According to Wu, the catalyst also operated for more than 1,000 hours at industry-level current densities of 1 and 2 amperes per square centimeter. That makes it one of the most durable platinum-free cathodes developed so far for anion-exchange membrane water electrolyzers.

"Our findings allowed us to rationalize the critical role of engineering the hydrogen-bond network at the catalyst/electrolyte interface in designing high-efficiency, low-cost AEMWEs," Wu said. "Our catalyst showed the lowest resistance across the studied potential range, which suggests the fastest hydrogen adsorption kinetics among the studied catalysts. This newly achieved performance and durability metrics make our catalyst one of the most promising membrane electrode assemblies for practical anion-exchange membrane water electrolyzers."

Potential for Large-Scale Hydrogen Production

Although the experiments were carried out at laboratory scale, the researchers plan to continue studying whether the technology can be expanded for industrial use.

The work was financially supported by G. Wu's startup fund at Washington University in St. Louis.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in developing a durable, platinum-free catalyst for clean hydrogen fuel production. This advancement has the potential to reduce costs and improve scalability of renewable hydrogen energy, which can broadly benefit society by enabling cleaner energy storage and reducing fossil fuel reliance. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights meaningful environmental impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create a cheaper catalyst to produce clean hydrogen fuel
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Clean hydrogen fuel is a promising energy source because it can store energy from wind, sun, or water. But making hydrogen usually needs expensive materials like platinum, which raises costs and limits use.

**What happened** – A team at Washington University in St. Louis, led by Professor Gang Wu, developed a new catalyst that doesn’t use platinum. Instead, it combines two cheaper materials, rhenium phosphide and molybdenum phosphide, to help split water into hydrogen and oxygen efficiently. This catalyst works in a special device called an anion-exchange membrane water electrolyzer (AEMWE).

**Impact** – This new catalyst is strong and can run for over 1,000 hours while producing hydrogen. It performs better than other expensive platinum-based catalysts. Because it is cheaper and durable, it could make clean hydrogen fuel more affordable and easier to produce on a larger scale.

**What’s next step** – The researchers want to test if this catalyst can be used in big industrial systems, not just in the lab. If successful, it might help produce clean hydrogen fuel for many different uses, from powering vehicles to storing renewable energy.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have made a durable, low-cost catalyst that could make clean hydrogen fuel cheaper and more accessible for everyday use.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent un catalyseur moins cher pour produire de l’hydrogène propre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’hydrogène propre est une source d’énergie prometteuse car il peut stocker l’énergie du vent, du soleil ou de l’eau. Mais sa production nécessite souvent des matériaux coûteux comme le platine, ce qui augmente les coûts et limite son utilisation.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de l’Université de Washington à St. Louis, dirigée par le professeur Gang Wu, a développé un nouveau catalyseur sans platine. Il combine deux matériaux moins chers, le phosphure de rhénium et le phosphure de molybdène, pour séparer efficacement l’eau en hydrogène et oxygène. Ce catalyseur fonctionne dans un appareil spécial appelé électrolyseur à membrane échangeuse d’anions (AEMWE).

**Impact** – Ce nouveau catalyseur est solide et peut fonctionner plus de 1 000 heures tout en produisant de l’hydrogène. Il est plus performant que les catalyseurs à base de platine plus coûteux. Grâce à son prix réduit et sa durabilité, il pourrait rendre l’hydrogène propre plus abordable et plus facile à produire à grande échelle.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent tester si ce catalyseur peut être utilisé dans des systèmes industriels à grande échelle, pas seulement en laboratoire. S’ils réussissent, cela pourrait aider à produire de l’hydrogène propre pour de nombreuses utilisations, comme alimenter des véhicules ou stocker l’énergie renouvelable.

**Phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont créé un catalyseur durable et peu coûteux qui pourrait rendre l’hydrogène propre moins cher et plus accessible pour un usage quotidien.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean un catalizador más barato para producir hidrógeno limpio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El hidrógeno limpio es una fuente de energía prometedora porque puede almacenar energía del viento, el sol o el agua. Pero producir hidrógeno suele necesitar materiales caros como el platino, lo que aumenta los costos y limita su uso.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de la Universidad de Washington en St. Louis, dirigido por el profesor Gang Wu, desarrolló un nuevo catalizador que no usa platino. En cambio, combina dos materiales más baratos, fosfuro de renio y fosfuro de molibdeno, para separar el agua en hidrógeno y oxígeno de forma eficiente. Este catalizador funciona en un dispositivo especial llamado electrólito de membrana de intercambio de aniones (AEMWE).

**Impacto** – Este nuevo catalizador es resistente y puede funcionar más de 1,000 horas produciendo hidrógeno. Rinde mejor que otros catalizadores a base de platino más caros. Por ser más barato y duradero, podría hacer que el hidrógeno limpio sea más accesible y fácil de producir a gran escala.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren probar si este catalizador puede usarse en sistemas industriales grandes, no solo en el laboratorio. Si tiene éxito, podría ayudar a producir hidrógeno limpio para muchas aplicaciones diferentes, desde alimentar vehículos hasta almacenar energía renovable.

**Frase clave** – Científicos han creado un catalizador duradero y económico que podría hacer el hidrógeno limpio más barato y accesible para el uso diario.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing water droplet splitting into two luminous streams—one blue symbolizing oxygen and one silver symbolizing hydrogen—flowing gently into a stylized, intricate catalyst structure made of interlocking crystalline shapes representing rhenium phosphide and molybdenum phosphide, set against a soft gradient background evoking clean renewable energy sources like sunlight and wind without depicting them directly.

articles.title: How nature-based solutions can help vulnerable and marginalized communities overcome climate injustice
articles.summary: Nature-based solutions led by Indigenous and local communities help address climate change impacts by restoring ecosystems, reducing disaster risks, strengthening livelihoods, supporting biodiversity, and advancing climate justice through sustainable local action.
articles.content:
This post is part of Global Voices’ May 2026 Spotlight series, “Global crisis, local solutions.” This series will offer stories of resistance and successful climate action, insight into how communities in the Global South are fighting back against the crisis, analysis of what this might mean for future generations, and more. You can support this coverage by donating here.

Nepal is one of the countries most vulnerable to the impacts of the climate crisis. Across the world, this crisis is causing widespread disruption and contributing to violations of basic rights, including the right to live with dignity, both directly and indirectly. As more and more global leaders recognize climate change as one of the greatest threats to human rights, climate justice is emerging as a fundamental human right.

In Nepal, climate change is impacting agriculture, biodiversity, and water and energy resources, and leading to increasingly common and severe hydrometeorological disasters, including floods, landslides, droughts, and other extreme weather events, as well as slow-onset climate change.

Climate change exacerbates the difficulties already faced by Indigenous communities, including political and economic marginalization, loss of land and resources, human rights violations, discrimination, and unemployment. Most of the Indigenous communities that are dependent on natural resources are affected by this environmental catastrophe.

Indigenous peoples are among the first to face the direct consequences of climate change. Climate injustice is even more severe for women, Dalits (lower caste individuals who face societal prejudice), Indigenous women, people with disabilities, and Madhesi communities, an ethnic minority in Nepal. They are further susceptible due to preexisting vulnerabilities driven by discrimination based on socially ascribed identities such as caste, ethnicity, and indigeneity, geographic remoteness, and socioeconomic marginalization. These entangled issues continue to widen existing inequalities in Nepal.

The climate crisis is also driving displacement and forcing many poor and vulnerable people, who are termed “climate refugees,” to migrate to urban areas due to floods, heat stress, drought, glacial lake outburst floods (GLOFs), and other extreme conditions.

Yet even as the disasters worsen, those most vulnerable to the effects of the climate crisis have the least say in how resources are allocated. For example, less than one percent of global climate finance reaches Indigenous populations, even though they play a crucial role in protecting natural resources and ecosystems.

Cases of Nature-Based Solutions (NbS)

Nature-based solutions (NbS) can be a self-help tool where ecosystem restoration in forests, wetlands, or mangroves can reduce disaster risks such as floods, landslides, and droughts in ways that generate both ecological and human benefits. These can be locally driven and community-based initiatives that further strengthen climate resilience, reduce dependence on imported fertilizers, and promote sustainable rural development.

In practical terms, NbS are increasingly used as context-specific measures to restore degraded ecosystems, stabilize slopes, and protect riverbanks. However, NbS can address climate injustice only if they are rights-based, locally led, and socially inclusive. Nature-based solutions are considered an affordable, inclusive option for managing climate change, disaster risk, and environmental deterioration while supporting biodiversity and local livelihoods.

Indigenous peoples and local communities play a vital role in climate mitigation and adaptation. One of their main roles is ecological stewardship: protecting forests, wetlands, and biodiversity. Many of these solutions already exist in practice; the Indigenous people are the original guardians of the ecosystem, who recognize the value of the forests and natural landscapes.

There are small-scale to medium-scale mitigation efforts carried out as nature-based solutions. For example, bioengineering techniques help mitigate flood impacts and safeguard vital ecosystem services like freshwater flow, soil stability, and habitat connectivity. More broadly, nature-based solutions can regulate water flow, reduce sediment loads, and strengthen ecosystem resilience through measures such as reforestation, wetland restoration, riverbank vegetation, and slope stabilization using bio-engineering techniques.

Shifting cultivation, a traditional, subsistence-based farming system practiced by Indigenous communities, is also an example of an NbS that is contributing to carbon sequestration in forests and therefore plays a role in climate mitigation.

NbS can also strengthen local economies through community forestry, agroforestry, and watershed restoration programs, which promote fisheries. Protecting critical water sources and farmland, restoring floodplains and wetlands can improve groundwater recharge, support fisheries, and provide habitats for wildlife. These processes enhance both ecological and economic resilience, empowering local people socially and economically through active participation in natural resource management.

Nature-based solutions (NbS) can be a new opportunity for green job creation, including roles such as forest guards, nursery workers, eco-guides, and timber processing workers. They can generate a new source of income for community forest groups, strengthen local green economies, and generate employment, especially for women and youth, for example, through compost production and sales.

NbS play a crucial role in mitigating flood impacts and safeguarding vital ecosystem services like freshwater flow, soil stability, and habitat connectivity. They take different forms and contribute not only to disaster risk reduction but also to the revival of local traditions, livelihood enhancement, and broader socioeconomic development. For example, Bio-dykes are an environmentally-friendly alternative to normal flood defense structures, where “normal” means huge concrete structures that are expensive to build, damage the environment, and are catastrophic when they fail. In contrast, the bio-dykes help bind soil and prevent erosion, maintaining the river’s original flow and preventing overflow during heavy rainfall. Bio-dykes deliver economic, environmental, and social benefits.

Bangladesh has another strong example of nature-based solutions (NbS). In the face of climate change, the country’s floating gardens offer an innovative solution to the challenges faced by millions of farmers. Similarly, mangrove forests serve as a nature-based solution for coastal flood protection, helping to reduce the impact of rising sea levels.

The example of Sudan also highlights the potential of NbS, particularly through bioengineering, water harvesting, and ecosystem restoration. These approaches have supported climate adaptation measures that contribute to water management and re-greening, which help communities resume farming activities. Integrated water resource management is helping communities restore the land in and beyond low-lying areas and natural water systems. Overall, these approaches aim to safeguard agricultural land, forests, and settlements from increasing risks of floods, landslides, and droughts driven by climate change.

Finally, converting forest waste into compost is another effective NbS that transforms fallen leaves, dry branches, and other organic material from community forests into usable compost. This practice reduces the risk of forest fires and offers a sustainable alternative to chemical fertilizers by enhancing soil fertility, improving moisture retention, and supporting microbial activity in the soil.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the significant positive impact of nature-based solutions led by Indigenous and local communities in addressing climate change and climate injustice. It details practical, scalable ecosystem restoration efforts that reduce disaster risks, support biodiversity, and strengthen local livelihoods, especially for vulnerable and marginalized groups. The story provides substantial context and examples from multiple countries, demonstrating broad societal and environmental benefits.
articles.personality_title: How nature-based solutions help vulnerable communities fight climate change
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Climate change is causing more floods, droughts, and other disasters, especially hurting Indigenous and marginalized communities in countries like Nepal. These groups often face discrimination and have fewer resources to protect themselves.

**What happened** – Indigenous and local communities are using nature-based solutions (NbS) to restore forests, wetlands, and riverbanks. These efforts reduce disaster risks like floods and landslides, improve soil and water quality, and create new jobs. Examples include Nepal’s forest restoration, Bangladesh’s floating gardens, and Sudan’s water harvesting techniques.

**Impact** – These nature-based methods help protect both the environment and people’s livelihoods. They reduce the need for costly concrete structures and harmful chemicals while supporting biodiversity and local economies. Vulnerable groups, including women and youth, gain new job opportunities and stronger community roles.

**What’s next step** – Expanding these locally led solutions with more support and funding can help more communities adapt to climate change. Sharing knowledge between countries and increasing climate finance for Indigenous peoples will strengthen these efforts.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Nature-based solutions driven by Indigenous and local communities offer practical, affordable ways to reduce climate risks and improve lives in vulnerable areas worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment les solutions basées sur la nature aident les communautés vulnérables à lutter contre le changement climatique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le changement climatique provoque davantage d’inondations, de sécheresses et d’autres catastrophes, affectant particulièrement les communautés autochtones et marginalisées dans des pays comme le Népal. Ces groupes subissent souvent des discriminations et disposent de moins de ressources pour se protéger.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les communautés autochtones et locales utilisent des solutions basées sur la nature (SbN) pour restaurer les forêts, les zones humides et les berges des rivières. Ces actions réduisent les risques de catastrophes comme les inondations et les glissements de terrain, améliorent la qualité du sol et de l’eau, et créent de nouveaux emplois. Parmi les exemples, on compte la restauration forestière au Népal, les jardins flottants au Bangladesh et les techniques de collecte d’eau au Soudan.

**Impact** – Ces méthodes naturelles protègent à la fois l’environnement et les moyens de subsistance des populations. Elles réduisent le besoin de structures coûteuses en béton et de produits chimiques nocifs tout en soutenant la biodiversité et les économies locales. Les groupes vulnérables, notamment les femmes et les jeunes, bénéficient de nouvelles opportunités d’emploi et d’un rôle renforcé dans la communauté.

**Prochaine étape** – Étendre ces solutions locales avec plus de soutien et de financement peut aider davantage de communautés à s’adapter au changement climatique. Le partage des connaissances entre pays et l’augmentation des financements climatiques pour les peuples autochtones renforceront ces efforts.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les solutions basées sur la nature menées par les communautés autochtones et locales offrent des moyens pratiques et abordables pour réduire les risques climatiques et améliorer la vie dans les zones vulnérables à travers le monde.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo las soluciones basadas en la naturaleza ayudan a comunidades vulnerables a enfrentar el cambio climático
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cambio climático está causando más inundaciones, sequías y otros desastres, afectando especialmente a las comunidades indígenas y marginadas en países como Nepal. Estos grupos a menudo enfrentan discriminación y tienen menos recursos para protegerse.

**Qué pasó** – Comunidades indígenas y locales están utilizando soluciones basadas en la naturaleza (SbN) para restaurar bosques, humedales y riberas de ríos. Estos esfuerzos reducen riesgos de desastres como inundaciones y deslizamientos, mejoran la calidad del suelo y el agua, y crean nuevos empleos. Ejemplos incluyen la restauración forestal en Nepal, jardines flotantes en Bangladesh y técnicas de recolección de agua en Sudán.

**Impacto** – Estos métodos naturales ayudan a proteger tanto el medio ambiente como los medios de vida de las personas. Reducen la necesidad de estructuras costosas de concreto y químicos dañinos, mientras apoyan la biodiversidad y las economías locales. Grupos vulnerables, incluyendo mujeres y jóvenes, obtienen nuevas oportunidades laborales y un papel más fuerte en la comunidad.

**Próximo paso** – Ampliar estas soluciones lideradas localmente con más apoyo y financiamiento puede ayudar a más comunidades a adaptarse al cambio climático. Compartir conocimientos entre países y aumentar el financiamiento climático para pueblos indígenas fortalecerá estos esfuerzos.

**Frase clave** – Las soluciones basadas en la naturaleza impulsadas por comunidades indígenas y locales ofrecen formas prácticas y accesibles para reducir riesgos climáticos y mejorar vidas en áreas vulnerables en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a lush Nepalese riverside landscape featuring gently sloping hills stabilized by woven bio-dykes of living plants, vibrant floating gardens gently bobbing on calm water, and silhouettes of diverse community tools like woven baskets and traditional farming implements resting nearby, all rendered in soft natural earth tones and greens to symbolize nature-based climate solutions empowering marginalized Indigenous and local communities.

articles.title: Ancient lost ocean may have built Central Asia’s dinosaur-era mountains
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered evidence that the vanished Tethys Ocean may have sculpted Central Asia’s mountainous landscape during the dinosaur era. Using decades of geological data, researchers found that distant tectonic activity linked to the ancient ocean appears to match periods of rapid mountain formation. Surprisingly, climate and mantle processes played only a minor role. The discovery could reshape how scientists understand mountain building across the planet.
articles.content:
A new study from Adelaide University suggests that the ancient Tethys Ocean played a major role in shaping Central Asia's landscape during the Cretaceous period, long before the rise of the Himalayas.

The research team reached this conclusion through a large-scale data analysis that combined hundreds of thermal history models collected from more than 30 years of geological studies across Central Asia.

Scientists have often linked the region's landscape to a combination of tectonic activity, climate changes, and processes deep within Earth's mantle over the past 250 million years. However, the new findings point to a different dominant force.

"We found that climate change and mantle processes had only little influence on the Central Asian landscape, which persisted in an arid climate for much of the last 250 million years," said Dr. Sam Boone, who was a post-doctoral researcher at Adelaide University when the research was conducted.

"Instead, the dynamics of the distant Tethys Ocean can directly be correlated with short-lived periods of mountain building in Central Asia."

How a Lost Ocean Influenced Mountain Building

The Tethys Ocean once stretched across a vast area of the planet before gradually disappearing during the Meso-Cenozoic period, which covers the last 250 million years. Today, the Mediterranean Sea is considered the final remnant of that ancient ocean.

"The present-day relief of Central Asia was largely built by the India-Eurasia collision and ongoing convergence," said co-author Associate Professor Stijn Glorie, from Adelaide University's School of Physics, Chemistry and Earth Sciences.

"However, during the Cretaceous periods, dinosaurs would have seen a mountainous landscape as well, similar to the present-day Basin-and-Range Province in the western USA.

"It is thought that the extension in the Tethys, due to roll-back of subducting slabs of ocean crust, reactivated old suture zones into a series of roughly parallel ridges in Central Asia, up to thousands of kilometers away from the Himalaya collision zone."

According to the researchers, geological activity connected to the ancient ocean may have triggered mountain formation far from the actual plate boundaries.

Thermal History Models Reveal Earth's Past

The study relied on thermal history models, which help scientists trace how rocks cooled as they moved closer to Earth's surface during periods of mountain uplift and erosion.

"These models were constructed using thermochronology methods and reveal how rocks cooled down when they are brought towards the surface during mountain uplift and subsequent erosion," Associate Professor Glorie said.

"We analyzed a compilation of thermal history models in function of plate-tectonic models for the Tethys Ocean evolution, as well as deep-time precipitation and mantle-convection models."

By combining these datasets, the team was able to reconstruct previously hidden chapters of Earth's geological history.

Applying the Research Beyond Central Asia

Associate Professor Glorie said the same research method could help scientists investigate other geological mysteries around the world. The study was published in Nature Communications Earth and Environment.

"There are many parts on the planet where the drivers and timing for mountain building and/or rifting are poorly understood. For example, closer to home, the break-up history of Australia from Antarctica is somewhat enigmatic," he said.

"Australia drifted away about 80 million years ago, but there is no obvious imprint of this in the thermal history record of either the Antarctic or Australian plate margins. Instead, they record much older cooling histories.

"We are applying the same approach as used in Central Asia to advance understanding of Australia-Antarctica break-up."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery that reshapes understanding of mountain building and Earth's geological history, using extensive data and innovative methods. This breakthrough has broad implications for geology and environmental science globally, offering new insights into tectonic processes and landscape formation.
articles.personality_title: Ancient ocean shaped Central Asia’s dinosaur-era mountains, new study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied how Central Asia’s mountains formed, usually linking them to tectonic plate movements, climate changes, and deep Earth processes over millions of years. The Tethys Ocean, which disappeared about 250 million years ago, once covered a large part of the region.

**What happened** – Researchers from Adelaide University analyzed over 30 years of geological data, including thermal history models that track how rocks cooled as they rose to the surface. They discovered that the Tethys Ocean’s movement played a key role in building Central Asia’s mountains during the dinosaur era (the Cretaceous period). This mountain-building happened far from the current plate boundaries and was less influenced by climate or deep Earth changes than previously thought.

**Impact** – This finding changes how scientists understand mountain formation, showing that ancient oceans can impact landscapes even at great distances. It also reveals that Central Asia had mountainous terrain long before the Himalayas formed, giving a new view of Earth’s history during the time of the dinosaurs.

**What’s next step** – The team plans to use the same methods to study other unclear geological events, such as how Australia separated from Antarctica millions of years ago. This approach could help explain mountain-building and rifting in other parts of the world where the causes are still unknown.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A lost ocean from dinosaur times shaped Central Asia’s mountains, revealing new details about Earth’s geological past and opening doors for future discoveries worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un océan ancien a façonné les montagnes d’Asie centrale à l’époque des dinosaures, révèle une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps la formation des montagnes d’Asie centrale, souvent attribuée aux mouvements des plaques tectoniques, aux changements climatiques et aux processus profonds de la Terre sur des millions d’années. L’océan Téthys, disparu il y a environ 250 millions d’années, couvrait autrefois une grande partie de la région.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université d’Adélaïde ont analysé plus de 30 ans de données géologiques, incluant des modèles d’histoire thermique qui suivent le refroidissement des roches en remontant à la surface. Ils ont découvert que le mouvement de l’océan Téthys a joué un rôle clé dans la formation des montagnes d’Asie centrale pendant l’ère des dinosaures (période du Crétacé). Cette formation de montagnes s’est produite loin des limites actuelles des plaques et a été moins influencée par le climat ou les changements profonds de la Terre que ce que l’on pensait.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change la compréhension de la formation des montagnes, montrant que des océans anciens peuvent influencer les paysages même à grande distance. Elle révèle aussi que l’Asie centrale avait un terrain montagneux bien avant la formation de l’Himalaya, offrant une nouvelle vision de l’histoire de la Terre à l’époque des dinosaures.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit d’utiliser la même méthode pour étudier d’autres événements géologiques peu clairs, comme la séparation de l’Australie et de l’Antarctique il y a des millions d’années. Cette approche pourrait aider à expliquer la formation des montagnes et le rifting dans d’autres régions du monde encore méconnues.

**Une phrase clé** – Un océan disparu de l’époque des dinosaures a façonné les montagnes d’Asie centrale, révélant de nouveaux détails sur le passé géologique de la Terre et ouvrant la voie à de futures découvertes dans le monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: Un océano antiguo moldeó las montañas de Asia Central en la era de los dinosaurios, revela estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo cómo se formaron las montañas de Asia Central, generalmente relacionándolas con movimientos de placas tectónicas, cambios climáticos y procesos profundos de la Tierra durante millones de años. El océano Tetis, que desapareció hace unos 250 millones de años, cubría gran parte de la región.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Adelaida analizaron más de 30 años de datos geológicos, incluyendo modelos de historia térmica que muestran cómo las rocas se enfriaron al acercarse a la superficie. Descubrieron que el movimiento del océano Tetis tuvo un papel importante en la formación de las montañas de Asia Central durante la era de los dinosaurios (período Cretácico). Esta formación de montañas ocurrió lejos de los límites actuales de las placas y fue menos influenciada por el clima o cambios profundos de la Tierra de lo que se pensaba.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo cambia la forma en que los científicos entienden la formación de montañas, mostrando que océanos antiguos pueden afectar paisajes a grandes distancias. También revela que Asia Central tenía un terreno montañoso mucho antes de que se formaran los Himalayas, ofreciendo una nueva visión de la historia de la Tierra en tiempos de dinosaurios.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea usar los mismos métodos para estudiar otros eventos geológicos poco claros, como la separación de Australia y la Antártida hace millones de años. Este enfoque podría ayudar a explicar la formación de montañas y el rifting en otras partes del mundo donde aún no se entienden bien las causas.

**Frase clave** – Un océano perdido de la era de los dinosaurios formó las montañas de Asia Central, revelando nuevos detalles sobre el pasado geológico de la Tierra y abriendo camino para futuros descubrimientos globales.
articles.image_prompt: A warm-toned detailed painting of an ancient vast ocean gently merging into a rugged mountainous landscape with layered ridges stretching across Central Asia, symbolized by flowing water transforming into rising rocky peaks under a soft, arid sky, with subtle thermal patterns glowing beneath the surface to represent deep geological forces shaping the land over millions of years.
